metadata.title: Markowitz Portfolio Optimizer — Efficient Frontier with Claude

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Salut, Liam — in for Bear. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Build it with a CLI, then take it apart?

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

Harry Markowitz asked: given a set of risky assets, what is the optimal blend? His 1952 paper gave the answer — and won the Nobel prize. The efficient frontier is the set of portfolios that give maximum return for a given level of risk. Below it, you are leaving return on the table. On it, you are optimal. Today we build the optimizer with Claude, find the minimum-variance and maximum-Sharpe portfolios, and draw the frontier.
beats.visual_intent: title card: risk-return space with frontier curve, individual assets as dots

beats.narration_text: Type the prompt. We ask Claude to write the optimizer: four assets with given expected returns and a covariance matrix with off-diagonal correlations of 0.3. Find minimum-variance and maximum-Sharpe portfolios using scipy.optimize.minimize. Generate 500 random feasible portfolios to fill out the opportunity set.

props.command:
claude "Write markowitz_optimizer.py: given expected_returns=[0.08,0.10,0.12,0.06]
and cov_matrix (4x4 with off-diagonal correlations ~0.3), use
scipy.optimize.minimize to find (a) minimum variance portfolio,
(b) max Sharpe ratio portfolio (rf=0.03). Print weights,
expected return, volatility, Sharpe. Generate 500 random portfolios."

beats.narration_text: Read it. Minimum variance: minimize w transpose times cov times w, subject to weights summing to one. Max Sharpe: minimize minus the Sharpe ratio — portfolio return minus risk-free, divided by portfolio volatility. Both are constrained quadratic programs solved by SLSQP. The covariance matrix does the heavy lifting.

props.code:
# markowitz_optimizer.py  —  Claude Code output
from scipy.optimize import minimize
import numpy as np

# Min-variance portfolio
min_var = minimize(
    lambda w: w @ cov @ w,
    w0,
    constraints=[{'type':'eq','fun':lambda w:sum(w)-1}],
    bounds=[(0,1)]*4,
    method='SLSQP'
)
# Max-Sharpe: minimize negative Sharpe
max_sharpe = minimize(
    lambda w: -(w@mu - rf) / np.sqrt(w@cov@w),
    w0, constraints=..., bounds=...
)

beats.narration_text: Run it. The grey dots are 500 random feasible portfolios — they fill the interior of the opportunity set. The crimson curve is the efficient frontier — the upper edge. The four individual assets are black dots. The blue star is the minimum-variance portfolio; the green star is the maximum-Sharpe ratio portfolio. Every point below the frontier is suboptimal.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: efficient frontier curve (crimson), feasible dots (grey), asset dots (black), min-var (blue), max-Sharpe (green) in risk-return space

beats.narration_text: What if you add a short-selling constraint and also require each weight to be at most 40 percent? The efficient frontier shifts — tighter constraints shrink the opportunity set. Ask Claude to rerun with the box constraints and compare the Sharpe ratios.

props.command:
claude "update markowitz_optimizer.py:
  -> bounds: each weight in [0, 0.4] (max 40% per asset)
  -> compare max-Sharpe before vs after constraint
  -> print the weight change and Sharpe ratio change"

beats.narration_text: The constrained frontier is inside the unconstrained one — constraints always hurt. The Sharpe ratio drops from 0.47 to 0.41. That is the cost of the constraint: 6 basis points of risk-adjusted return. Every real portfolio pays this cost — no fund manager can hold the mathematical optimum.
beats.visual_intent: summary card: two frontiers overlaid, Sharpe ratio comparison

beats.narration_text: Your move: extend to real assets. Pull historical returns from yfinance for a 10-asset portfolio and run the optimizer. The estimated covariance matrix will be noisy — try the Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage estimator from sklearn to stabilize it.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: real assets, Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage, sklearn covariance

beats.narration_text: Then add factor exposure constraints. Require the portfolio to be beta-neutral versus the market — add a linear constraint that the weighted-average beta equals zero. The frontier shifts again, and the constrained optimum is now factor-clean.
beats.visual_intent: code snippet: beta-neutral constraint added to minimize() call

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. Your move: extend to real assets. Pull historical returns from yfinance for a 10-asset portfolio and run the optimizer. The estimated covariance matrix will be noisy — try the Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage estimator from sklearn to stabilize it. Then add factor exposure constraints. Require the portfolio to be beta-neutral versus the market — add a linear constraint that the weighted-average beta equals zero. The frontier shifts again, and the constrained optimum is now factor-clean.

props.artifactHeading: Markowitz Portfolio Optimizer — Efficient Frontier with Claude
artifactLines: The constrained frontier is inside the unconstrained one — constraints always hurt.
Your move: extend to real assets.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Markowitz Portfolio Optimizer — Efficient Frontier with Claude] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?

props.title: Markowitz Portfolio Optimizer — Efficient Frontier with Claude
props.subline: the frontier shifts again, and the constrained optimum is now factor-clean
